nodes.name: Opening Lines
nodes.description: Famous first lines of novels and works of literature

exercises.name: Famous Opening Lines — Identify the Work

items.prompt: Which novel begins with the sentence 'Call me Ishmael'?
items.explanation:
Opening line of Herman Melville's *Moby-Dick* (1851); the most famous opening in American literature
Ishmael is the narrator; the name is biblical (son of Abraham, outcast) and signals the theme of alienation
The actual first sentence technically in some editions is longer, but 'Call me Ishmael' stands alone as the canonical opener
Melville published the novel to commercial failure; it was rediscovered in the 1920s
The novel's last line is equally famous: '...and the great shroud of the sea rolled on as it rolled five thousand years ago'

items.prompt: Which Dickens novel begins 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times, it was the age of wisdom, it was the age of foolishness'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Charles Dickens's *A Tale of Two Cities* (1859); set during the French Revolution in London and Paris
The sentence continues for six more balanced contrasts ('it was the epoch of belief, it was the epoch of incredulity...') and ends: 'in short, the period was so far like the present period'
One of the best-selling novels of all time with an estimated 200 million copies sold
Contains Sydney Carton's famous last words: 'It is a far, far better thing that I do, than I have ever done'
Published as a serial in Dickens's own journal *All the Year Round* (1859)
items.cardFront: 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times...'

items.prompt: Which Tolstoy novel begins 'Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Leo Tolstoy's *Anna Karenina* (1878); published serially 1875–1877
The novel follows the doomed affair of the married Anna Karenina and the cavalry officer Count Vronsky
Anna throws herself under a train at the end — one of literature's most famous tragic endings
The opening line (known as the 'Tolstoy principle' in social science) has generated significant academic debate about whether it's actually true
Tolstoy considered *Anna Karenina* his first true novel; he dismissed *War and Peace* as 'not a novel'
items.cardFront: 'Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way.'

items.prompt: Which Austen novel begins 'It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Jane Austen's *Pride and Prejudice* (1813); one of the most quoted sentences in English literature
The irony is immediate: the 'truth' is clearly held by Mrs. Bennet, not the universe; the sentence mocks her obsession with marrying off her daughters
Follows Elizabeth Bennet and Mr. Darcy in the prototypical enemies-to-lovers romance
Original working title was *First Impressions*, submitted to a publisher in 1797 and rejected unread
Austen published all six novels anonymously, credited only to 'A Lady'
items.cardFront: 'It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.'

items.prompt: Which novel begins 'It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen'?
items.explanation:
Opening of George Orwell's *1984* (1949); 'striking thirteen' immediately signals a world where normal reality has been distorted
Orwell's real name was Eric Arthur Blair; written on the Scottish island of Jura while dying of tuberculosis
Introduced 'Big Brother,' 'doublethink,' 'Newspeak,' and 'Room 101' into the English language
Sales spiked after the Snowden leaks in 2013 and again in 2017 after the 'alternative facts' controversy
The title comes from reversing the last two digits of 1948, the year Orwell completed the manuscript
items.cardFront: 'It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen.'

items.prompt: Which gothic novel begins 'Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Daphne du Maurier's *Rebecca* (1938); the narrator is the unnamed second wife of widower Maxim de Winter
Manderley is the imposing Cornwall estate haunted by the memory of the first wife, Rebecca
The narrator is never named in the entire novel — a deliberate technique to show how thoroughly she is overshadowed
Alfred Hitchcock's 1940 film adaptation won the Academy Award for Best Picture
Sold over 3 million copies in its first decade; still in print continuously since publication
items.cardFront: 'Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again.'

items.prompt: Which novella begins 'As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams he found himself transformed in his bed into a gigantic insect'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Franz Kafka's *The Metamorphosis* (*Die Verwandlung*, 1915); published in German
Kafka never specifies the insect; the German 'Ungeziefer' means 'vermin' — the word 'insect' is a translator's interpretation
Gregor continues thinking about work even after his transformation; the horror is his family's cold rejection more than the transformation itself
Kafka asked his friend Max Brod to burn all his manuscripts after his death; Brod published them instead
Kafka died of tuberculosis in 1924 at age 40, never knowing his posthumous fame
The word 'Kafkaesque' (denoting absurd, nightmarish bureaucracy) entered English as a direct result of this and related works
items.cardFront: '...he found himself transformed in his bed into a gigantic insect.'

items.prompt: Which novel begins 'Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, General Aureliano Buendía was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Gabriel García Márquez's *One Hundred Years of Solitude* (1967); the defining sentence of magical realism
The ice is an astonishing novelty in the tropical village of Macondo; the juxtaposition of firing squad and wonder sets the novel's entire tone
García Márquez reportedly said the entire novel came to him fully formed when he heard this first sentence in his head; he turned his car around and wrote for 18 months
Sold 30+ million copies in 35+ languages; first Latin American novel to win the Booker International Prize
García Márquez won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982
items.cardFront: '...as he faced the firing squad, General Aureliano Buendía was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice.'

items.prompt: Which Nabokov novel begins 'Lolita, light of my life, fire of my loins. Lo-lee-ta'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Vladimir Nabokov's *Lolita* (1955); the alliterative triple is among the most famous openings in 20th-century fiction
The narrator Humbert Humbert is a pedophile whose ornate prose style is designed to seduce the reader as well as his victim
Nabokov deliberately makes Humbert charming and Lolita's perspective invisible — the reader must work to recognize the abuse
Refused by four American publishers; first published by the Olympia Press in Paris (which specialized in erotica); eventually published by Putnam in the U.S. in 1958 to enormous sales
Nabokov was a lepidopterist (butterfly expert) and composed the novel in English, his third language after Russian and French
items.cardFront: 'Lolita, light of my life, fire of my loins.'

items.prompt: Which Vonnegut novel begins 'All this happened, more or less'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Kurt Vonnegut's *Slaughterhouse-Five* (1969); the casual qualifier 'more or less' immediately signals the novel's relationship with fact and fiction
Subtitled *The Children's Crusade: A Duty-Dance with Death*; based on Vonnegut's own survival of the firebombing of Dresden as a POW
Featuring time-traveler Billy Pilgrim and the phrase 'So it goes' after every mention of death — used 106 times
The novel ends with the direct statement 'I was there' — Vonnegut himself appears as a character
Banned from many U.S. school districts; one school board burned copies in 1973 (an action Vonnegut wrote about mordantly)

items.prompt: Which Joyce novel begins 'Stately, plump Buck Mulligan came from the stairhead, bearing a bowl of lather on which a mirror and a razor lay crossed'?
items.explanation:
Opening of James Joyce's *Ulysses* (1922); the novel is set entirely on a single day, June 16, 1904
Buck Mulligan is based on Oliver St John Gogarty, with whom Joyce shared a Martello tower in Sandycove — the setting of this opening
June 16 is celebrated annually as 'Bloomsday' in Dublin; fans re-enact Leopold Bloom's journey through the city
Banned in the U.S. for obscenity until the 1933 landmark court ruling *United States v. One Book Called Ulysses*
Ranked #1 on the Modern Library's list of the 100 Best English-Language Novels of the 20th Century
items.cardFront: 'Stately, plump Buck Mulligan came from the stairhead...'

items.prompt: Which Toni Morrison novel begins '124 was spiteful. Full of a baby's venom'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Toni Morrison's *Beloved* (1987); '124' is the address of the haunted house where the novel begins
The number 124 appears as each chapter opens, changing from 'spiteful' to 'loud' to 'quiet' as the haunting evolves
Based on the true story of Margaret Garner, an enslaved woman who killed her daughter rather than let her be returned to slavery
Won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1988; Morrison won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993
In a 2006 *New York Times* survey, named the best American novel of the past 25 years
items.cardFront: '124 was spiteful. Full of a baby's venom.'

items.prompt: Which William Gibson novel begins 'The sky above the port was the color of television, tuned to a dead channel'?
items.explanation:
Opening of William Gibson's *Neuromancer* (1984); the definitive opening line of cyberpunk fiction
Coined the term 'cyberspace' and introduced the concept of 'the matrix' (a virtual reality network) years before the internet was widely used
First novel to win the Hugo, Nebula, and Philip K. Dick Awards — the triple crown of science fiction
The grey static of a dead TV channel stood for alienation and information overload in 1984; a generation raised on screens now may read it as simply 'grey'
Gibson has said the line came to him as a precise observation; he was living in Vancouver and working on a manual typewriter
items.cardFront: 'The sky above the port was the color of television, tuned to a dead channel.'

items.prompt: Which Twain novel begins 'You don't know about me without you have read a book by the name of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer; but that ain't no matter'?
items.explanation:
Opening of Mark Twain's *Adventures of Huckleberry Finn* (1884); the narrator Huck directly addresses the reader in his own vernacular voice
The opening immediately establishes Huck's authentic dialect and self-awareness — he is not a reliable educated narrator
Ernest Hemingway wrote: 'All modern American literature comes from one book by Mark Twain called *Huckleberry Finn*'
Frequently banned in American schools, both in the 19th century for being 'low' and in the 20th for its racial language
Covers Huck and Jim's journey down the Mississippi River; a sustained critique of slavery and Southern society
items.cardFront: 'You don't know about me without you have read a book by the name of *The Adventures of Tom Sawyer*...'

items.prompt: Which Fitzgerald novel begins 'In my younger and more vulnerable years my father gave me some advice that I've been turning over in my mind ever since'?
items.explanation:
Opening of F. Scott Fitzgerald's *The Great Gatsby* (1925); narrated by Nick Carraway, who recalls Gatsby's story years later
Nick's father tells him to remember that 'a sense of the fundamental decencies is parcelled out unequally at birth' — a loaded observation for a novel about class
Published to mixed reviews and modest sales; became a canonical American novel only after WWII when Armed Services editions circulated widely
The green light at the end of Daisy's dock — symbol of Gatsby's longing — is one of literature's most analyzed images
Fitzgerald coined the term 'Jazz Age'; he died in 1940 at 44, believing himself a failure
items.cardFront: 'In my younger and more vulnerable years my father gave me some advice...'
